questions.stem: A 6-month-old boy is brought to the GP because his weight has dropped from the 50th to the 9th centile over 4 months. He is exclusively breastfed, appears well, and has no vomiting or diarrhoea. Examination is normal. Parents seem stressed and report his feeds are 'fussy'.
questions.explanation: Normal examination and absence of GI symptoms point away from organic causes; the history of fussy feeding and parental stress suggests non-organic FTT.

questions.stem: A 2-year-old girl presents to A&E with a 2-day history of increased work of breathing and cough. She has a history of recurrent chest infections and her weight is below the 0.4th centile. On exam, she is tachypnoeic with coarse crackles. Her stools are noted to be offensive.
questions.explanation: The combination of recurrent chest infections, failure to thrive, and offensive stools (steatorrhoea) is classic for CF; sweat test is diagnostic.

questions.stem: A 9-month-old girl with a known ventricular septal defect presents with poor weight gain over the last 2 months despite being on diuretics. She is tachycardic and has a palpable liver edge.
options: Worsening heart failure due to VSD
questions.explanation: Tachycardia, hepatomegaly, and failure to thrive indicate decompensation; in a known VSD, this is most likely due to worsening left-to-right shunt.

questions.stem: A 4-month-old ex-premature infant (born at 28 weeks) is noted to have weight gain velocity below the 5th centile since discharge from the neonatal unit. He has no vomiting or diarrhoea. He is bottle-fed with standard term formula. Examination shows mild tachypnoea and increased work of breathing.
questions.explanation: In ex-premature infants, BPD is a common cause of increased metabolic demand and respiratory effort leading to poor weight gain; reflux and allergy are less likely without GI symptoms.

questions.stem: A 1-year-old boy is referred for poor growth. His weight has dropped from the 25th to the 2nd centile over 6 months. He is noted to be constipated, with a large anterior fontanelle and umbilical hernia.
questions.explanation: Constipation, large fontanelle, umbilical hernia, and failure to thrive are classic signs of hypothyroidism; newborn screening can miss some cases.

questions.stem: A 14-month-old boy is referred for falling weight. He was born at term, normal birth weight. His weight has crossed from the 50th to the 9th centile over the past 4 months. He has mild abdominal distension and loose stools. What is the most appropriate first-line investigation to identify an organic cause?
questions.explanation: Coeliac disease is a common treatable cause of failure to thrive in this age group; TTG IgA has high sensitivity and specificity. Full blood count may show anaemia but is less specific. Sweat test is for cystic fibrosis but is not first-line without respiratory symptoms or meconium ileus.

questions.stem: A 6-week-old infant presents with severe failure to thrive, weight <0.4th centile, with vomiting, diarrhoea, and metabolic alkalosis. What is the best next investigation to confirm the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Cystic fibrosis is a 'never-miss' cause of severe failure to thrive with dehydration and metabolic alkalosis (pseudo-Bartter syndrome). Sweat chloride test is the diagnostic gold standard. Urinary organic acids would be considered for metabolic disorders but are less likely given the presentation.

questions.stem: A 9-month-old ex-34-week premature infant is seen in clinic. Current weight is 7.1 kg. On the UK-WHO growth chart for corrected age, this corresponds to the 2nd centile. Which of the following growth parameters most clearly defines 'failure to thrive' in this child?
options: Weight crossing more than two major centile spaces downwards
Weight more than 2 standard deviations below the mean for chronological age
Weight gain less than expected over 3 months
questions.explanation: In UK practice, using corrected age for prematurity, a weight below the 0.4th centile is a clear definition of failure to thrive. Crossing centiles is also concerning but requires longitudinal data; the 0.4th centile is a specific threshold.

questions.stem: A 2-year-old girl with spastic quadriplegic cerebral palsy and gastrostomy feeding presents with poor weight gain over 6 months. She has no vomiting or diarrhoea. What is the most practical and validated measure to assess her nutritional status?
questions.explanation: MUAC is a simple, reliable, and validated measure of nutritional status in children with disabilities, as it is less affected by muscle wasting or contractures. Skinfold thickness can be difficult in spasticity; DEXA is not routinely available. Prealbumin is an acute-phase reactant.

questions.stem: A 5-month-old infant with acute severe failure to thrive presents to the emergency department with lethargy, sunken eyes, and poor skin turgor. Initial capillary blood gas shows pH 7.1, base excess -15, sodium 150 mmol/L, potassium 5.8 mmol/L. Which investigation is most appropriate to confirm the cause of the dehydration?
questions.explanation: Given hypernatraemic dehydration, urine osmolality helps differentiate renal causes (e.g., diabetes insipidus) from extrarenal losses. Serum osmolality also useful but urine osmolality directly assesses concentrating ability. Stool culture is relevant if diarrhoea is present but not stated.

questions.stem: A 9-month-old infant presents with failure to thrive. Weight crosses from 25th to 2nd centile over six months. Examination is normal; investigations including full blood count, coeliac serology, urine culture, and thyroid function are unremarkable. Social history reveals maternal depression and poor feeding interaction. What is the most appropriate first-line management?
options: Referral to paediatric dietitian for dietary advice
Prescription of cyproheptadine as an appetite stimulant
Multidisciplinary team input including health visitor, social worker, and parenting support
questions.explanation: Non-organic failure to thrive is primarily managed by addressing the underlying psychosocial factors. Multidisciplinary team support is first-line, not dietary manipulation or hospital admission. The most tempting distractor is high-calorie formula, but without addressing the root cause, this is unlikely to be effective.

questions.stem: An 18-month-old child presents with failure to thrive, chronic diarrhoea, and abdominal distension. Coeliac serology (anti-tissue transglutaminase antibodies) is strongly positive. A subsequent duodenal biopsy confirms villous atrophy. What is the definitive first-line treatment?
questions.explanation: Coeliac disease requires a strict gluten-free diet to promote intestinal healing and normal growth. A gluten challenge is contraindicated before diagnosis is established. Vitamin supplementation is adjunctive, not primary. Corticosteroids are reserved for severe refractory cases.

questions.stem: A 2-year-old child from a deprived area presents with severe wasting, pedal oedema, and apathy. Mid-upper arm circumference is 11 cm; weight-for-height less than -3 SD. A diagnosis of severe acute malnutrition (marasmic kwashiorkor) is made. What is the most important first-line management step?
options: Initiate full-strength infant formula ad libitum to correct caloric deficit rapidly
Start small, frequent feeds with cautious advancement and close electrolyte monitoring to prevent refeeding syndrome
Intravenous 50% dextrose bolus for hypoglycaemia prevention
questions.explanation: In severe acute malnutrition, refeeding syndrome is a major risk. Initial management involves cautious feeding with small volumes (e.g., F75 formula), monitoring phosphate, potassium, and magnesium. High-calorie feeds ad libitum, albumin, or diuretics are dangerous. The most tempting distractor is rapid refeeding, which can be fatal.

questions.stem: A 6-year-old child with cystic fibrosis (homozygous F508del) has been on increasing caloric intake but fails to gain weight. He passes greasy, foul-smelling stools. Faecal elastase is decreased (<15 μg/g). What is the first-line therapy to improve nutritional status?
options: Pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy (PERT) with each meal and snack
Fat-soluble vitamin supplementation (A, D, E, K)
questions.explanation: Exocrine pancreatic insufficiency causing malabsorption is a common CF complication. PERT is the cornerstone, enabling digestion and absorption of fat and protein. High-calorie diet alone will not correct malabsorption. Vitamin supplementation is adjunctive. Ursodeoxycholic acid is for liver disease; dornase alfa for lung disease.

questions.stem: A 10-day-old neonate born at term presents with poor feeding, prolonged jaundice, a large anterior fontanelle, and failure to gain weight since birth. The newborn screening heel-prick test shows a low T4 and high TSH. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
options: Oral levothyroxine plus subcutaneous growth hormone
questions.explanation: Congenital hypothyroidism is a medical emergency requiring immediate thyroid hormone replacement to prevent neurodevelopmental delay. Levothyroxine alone is indicated. Growth hormone is not needed. Radioactive iodine or antithyroid drugs would worsen hypothyroidism. Iodine is rarely deficient in the UK.

questions.stem: A 6-month-old boy is brought to the GP by his mother because he has not gained weight for 2 months. He was born at term, birth weight 3.2 kg. Currently weight 6.2 kg (0.4th centile). He is alert, feeding well, no vomiting or diarrhoea. Examination normal. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Take a detailed feeding history and plot growth on a centile chart.
Advise mother to increase calorie intake and review in 2 weeks.
Check full blood count, U&Es, coeliac screen.
questions.explanation: In a well child with faltering growth, the first step is to take a detailed feeding history and plot serial measurements (NICE guidance). Urine dip and bloods are second-line if history unclear or red flags. Distractor B (advising increased calories) is premature without a proper history.

questions.stem: A 2-year-old girl with faltering growth (weight 10 kg, below 2nd centile, down from 25th at 1 year) is seen in clinic. She has a normal appetite but prefers liquids. Developmental milestones normal. Examination unremarkable. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Refer to speech and language therapy.
Arrange hospital admission for nasogastric feeding.
Request a 3-day food diary and provide feeding advice.
questions.explanation: For mild-moderate faltering growth without red flags, first step is dietary intervention (food diary and advice). Supplements (A) are not first-line; admission (C) is for severe cases; toxicology (D) not indicated; speech therapy (E) only if swallowing issues.

questions.stem: An 8-month-old girl is brought to ED with poor feeding, lethargy, and weight loss. She was previously healthy but now weight 5.5 kg (well below 0.4th centile), dry mucous membranes, sunken eyes, and reduced skin turgor. Capillary refill 3 seconds. Parents say she has been vomiting occasionally. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Refer immediately to paediatric intensive care.
Obtain a chest X-ray to exclude pneumonia.
Administer a normal saline bolus 20 ml/kg intravenously.
Check serum electrolytes, calcium, phosphate, and magnesium.
Start nasogastric feeds with a high-calorie formula.
questions.explanation: The child shows signs of clinical dehydration and shock; immediate resuscitation with IV fluids is priority (APLS guidelines). Electrolytes (C) are important but after stabilization; NG feeds (A) contraindicated in shock; chest X-ray (D) not urgent; PICU referral (E) not first step.

questions.stem: A 10-month-old infant with severe malnutrition (weight 4.5 kg, less than 0.4th centile) is admitted for refeeding. She has been failing to thrive due to neglect. Initial bloods show low phosphate, low potassium, and normal magnesium. She is started on nasogastric feeds. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Continue feeds at reduced rate and monitor electrolytes closely.
Transfer to paediatric intensive care for cardiac monitoring.
Stop the feeds immediately and give IV phosphate.
Add potassium and phosphate supplements to the feeds.
Give a bolus of IV calcium gluconate.
questions.explanation: In refeeding syndrome, management is to continue feeds at a reduced rate (e.g., 50% of target) and monitor/correct electrolytes. Stopping feeds (A) worsens malnutrition; supplements (C) are given but not first step; PICU (D) not needed unless arrhythmias; calcium (E) not indicated here.

questions.stem: A 9-month-old ex-preterm infant (born at 28 weeks) has failure to thrive. He is on home oxygen for bronchopulmonary dysplasia and has been struggling with oral feeds. Weight is 6 kg (3rd centile for corrected age). He has no acute respiratory distress. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Start a proton pump inhibitor for presumed gastro-oesophageal reflux.
Refer to a paediatric dietitian for high-calorie feeding.
Increase his home oxygen flow rate to improve feeding endurance.
questions.explanation: In ex-preterm infants with BPD and poor weight gain, nutritional support is key; a dietitian can advise on calorie fortification. Oxygen (A) not indicated if saturations normal; swallow study (C) if aspiration suspected but not first step; PPI (D) only with reflux symptoms; growth hormone (E) not first-line.

questions.stem: A 6-month-old infant is brought to the GP by his mother because of poor weight gain. The health visitor notes that his weight has dropped from the 50th centile to the 2nd centile over the past 4 months. He is otherwise well, with no vomiting or diarrhoea. Which single finding from the growth chart is the strongest red flag indicating severe failure to thrive?
questions.explanation: NICE defines severe failure to thrive as weight below the 0.4th centile or crossing more than two major centile spaces. Crossing from 50th to 2nd is more than two spaces, but weight below 0.4th is an absolute threshold that demands urgent referral.

questions.stem: An 18-month-old girl is seen in the paediatric outpatient clinic for poor weight gain. Her weight has dropped from the 50th centile to the 9th centile. On examination she appears well and is developmentally appropriate. Which additional growth parameter finding would raise immediate suspicion for an organic cause of failure to thrive?
options: Body mass index between 5th and 10th centile
Mid-upper arm circumference on the 25th centile
questions.explanation: A deceleration in head circumference (crossing centiles downward) is a red flag for intracranial pathology such as brain tumour, metabolic disease, or chronic infection. This warrants urgent neuroimaging and investigation.

questions.stem: A 6-week-old infant presents with poor feeding, lethargy, and failure to thrive since birth. There is no fever, but the baby is noted to have a weak cry and poor tone. Which of the following life-threatening conditions is the most important to exclude?
questions.explanation: Urinary tract infection in neonates can present with non-specific symptoms like poor feeding, lethargy, and poor weight gain. It is a common cause of sepsis in this age group and must be ruled out with a urine culture.

questions.stem: A 14-month-old child is admitted with severe failure to thrive (weight below 0.4th centile). He is started on nasogastric feeds. After 48 hours, which laboratory finding is most concerning for a life-threatening complication of feeding?
questions.explanation: Refeeding syndrome in severely malnourished children can cause dangerous hypophosphataemia, leading to respiratory and cardiac failure. Serum phosphate should be monitored closely.

questions.stem: An 8-month-old infant presents with a 2-month history of faltering weight and intermittent vomiting. On examination, there is sunsetting eye sign and a bulging fontanelle. What is the most likely underlying diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Sunsetting eyes and bulging fontanelle indicate raised intracranial pressure, and vomiting with failure to thrive can be presenting features of a brain tumour. Urgent neuroimaging is needed.
